Learning ThinkPHP from Scratch Day 9 - Writing Your First Controller

Now that we've got a handle on the MVC architecture, it's time to actually start writing some code. The first piece we'll tackle is the Controller. But before we get into that, there are a few settings we should change first so development goes a lot smoother.
Before you do anything else, I'd really recommend turning on debug mode first. If you don't, you're going to have a rough time tracking down problems (kind of like writing JavaScript without using

to debug). Once it's on, you'll get much more detailed error messages, which makes it way clearer where things are going wrong during development. Just remember: when you deploy your project, make sure you turn debug mode back off. ↓
Turning it on is easy. Just open
in your project directory, search for
. That's it, debug mode is on. ↓

To kick things off, we'll learn how to define a Controller. So what does "defining" mean here? Think of it like gathering the materials before you build something. If we use JavaScript as an analogy, it's basically like creating a
inside it, then create another folder called
inside that (careful, lowercase c, not uppercase C). Then create an

<?php
namespace app\about\controller;

class Index
{
    public function index()
    {
        return 'About!';
    }
}
One thing to watch out for here is the
. It defines the namespace, so don't use the default
. If you don't do this, you'll run into conflicts, and an incorrect
will also cause "Controller not found" errors. ↓
And that's the basic starting point for defining a Controller. Pretty simple, right?
But like I mentioned earlier, at this stage of development we're using Routes to access URLs. The Route definition part will come later.

Back when we covered the directory structure, I briefly touched on the structure of URLs. The main idea is that URL access is decided through Routes. So let me say a bit more about how URL access works here.
By default, ThinkPHP's Controller URL access rule looks like this:
part can be omitted, so you only need to type
. But if we want to access the
. Of course, you can also just type
But what if there's another Controller under

<?php
namespace app\about\controller;

class HelloWorld
{
    public function Hello()
    {
        return 'hello，world！';
    }
}
In that case, the URL to access it would be
When ThinkPHP converts URLs, uppercase letters get converted to lowercase, and an uppercase letter sitting in the middle of a name gets converted to an underscore

So one thing we've noticed is that you can actually render a page for the user using just the Controller, without going through the View. But doing it that way doesn't really fit the MVC design pattern. So this post is just an intro to the basic ways of defining and working with a Controller. There's a lot more Controller writing to come.

Now that we understand the MVC architecture, it's time to start writing some code. The first thing we'll write is the Controller. But before we get there, there are a couple of settings we need to tweak so development goes a lot smoother. In this post we'll turn on debug mode, define a basic Controller, and walk through how ThinkPHP maps URLs to your controllers.
